payload.answer: Knowledge graphs are structured networks of real-world entities connected by relationships, stored as nodes and edges in a graph model. They support flexible queries, inference, and integration across datasets. Typical building blocks include: 1) Entities (nodes) with attributes, 2) Relationships (edges) with predicates, 3) Schemas or ontologies to define types and constraints, and 4) Provenance and context to enable trust. Common use cases: data integration across sources, semantic search, disambiguation and entity resolution, recommendations, and enriching data with context. Key technologies include RDF/OWL, property graphs, SPARQL, Cypher, and graph databases (e.g., Neo4j, Blazegraph, Stardog, Amazon Neptune). Starting points to learn: introductory tutorials on knowledge graphs, tutorials on RDF and SPARQL, practical guides to building a small graph from sample datasets, and exploring open knowledge graphs like Wikidata or DBpedia.

memory_cards.statement: A knowledge graph is a structured representation of real-world entities (nodes) and their interrelations (edges), typically using a graph data model to enable flexible querying, reasoning, and integration across data silos.
Common use cases include data integration, semantic search, recommendations, and enriching data with contextual relationships.
